coredata.dc:title: Transparent and flexible ultraviolet photoconductors based on solution-processed graphene quantum dots on reduced graphene oxide films
coredata.dc:creator: Thanh, Do Trong
Ko, Kang Bok
Khurelbaatar, Zagarzusem
Choi, Chel-Jong
Hong, Chang-Hee
Cuong, Tran Viet
coredata.dc:description: A combination of graphene quantum dots and chemically reduced graphene oxide films was obtained by spray-coating their dispersions onto an indium tin oxide coated polyethylene terephthalate substrate. Herein, the graphene quantum dots played a major role in the absorption of ultraviolet light and generation of carriers, whereas the reduced graphene films acted as the transparent electron pathway network. Consequently, the fabricated structure showed good transparency and flexibility, and achieved noticeable ultraviolet sensing. The facile, inexpensive, and extremely low temperature techniques used can be easily upgraded to a larger scale and are compatible with other substrates. In addition, a combination of reduced graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots offers a potential graphene-based platform for foldable optoelectronic device applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A. Nanostructures
A. Thin films
B. Chemical synthesis
B. Optical properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication procedure for flexible transparent UV sensor using solution-processed GQDs on rGO films.
HRTEM images of (a) GQDs with the size distribution and (b) GQD, Inset: the lattice spacing of GQD measured from the blue rectangle.

(a) PL spectra of GQDs, rGO and GQDs on rGO film, respectively. Inset: GQDs dispersed in water before and after illuminated under UV lamp and (b) Transmittance spectra of rGO and GQDs on rGO. Inset: image of fabricated UV sensor with 1×1cm2 active area.

(a) Typical I–V characteristic of the fabricated device measured in the dark and under 365nm UV LED illumination (0.5mW/cm2) and (b) Photocurrent of fabricated device in response to different power of 365nm UV LED illumination.
(a) Photoresponsivity of fabricated device at 1V bias under illumination with different wavelength and (b) The mechanism for UV sensing of GQDs/rGO film.

head.ce:title: Transparent and flexible ultraviolet photoconductors based on solution-processed graphene quantum dots on reduced graphene oxide films

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Semiconductor Physics Research Center, School of Semiconductor and Chemical Engineering, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju, 561-756, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Semiconductor and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NTT Hi-Tech Institute, Nguyen Tat Thanh University, 298-300 A Nguyen Tat Thanh Street, Ho Chi Minh City, Viet Nam

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: NTT Hi-Tech Institute, Nguyen Tat Thanh University, 298-300 A Nguyen Tat Thanh Street, Ho Chi Minh City, Viet Nam.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A combination of graphene quantum dots and chemically reduced graphene oxide films was obtained by spray-coating their dispersions onto an indium tin oxide coated polyethylene terephthalate substrate. Herein, the graphene quantum dots played a major role in the absorption of ultraviolet light and generation of carriers, whereas the reduced graphene films acted as the transparent electron pathway network. Consequently, the fabricated structure showed good transparency and flexibility, and achieved noticeable ultraviolet sensing. The facile, inexpensive, and extremely low temperature techniques used can be easily upgraded to a larger scale and are compatible with other substrates. In addition, a combination of reduced graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots offers a potential graphene-based platform for foldable optoelectronic device applications.

ce:section.ce:para: There has been much interest in transparent flexible ultraviolet (UV) sensors owing to their foldable, wearable, lightweight, and portable characteristics as compared to the conventional rigid sensors [1]. Although various wide-bandgap semiconductors such as nitride compounds and metal oxides have been exploited for use in high performance UV sensors, the most active layer materials are not compatible with flexible substrates. The studies on flexible UV sensor detectors have often employed low dimensional inorganic nanostructures; however, they have a low transmittance, and their preparation processes and device integration are difficult. Consequently, their large scale production has been impeded [2,3].

Owing to the nature of the flexible substrate, the fabrication of transparent and flexible UV sensors is subject to several limitations, such as low processing temperatures and high surface roughness of the films [4]. As such, many conventional semiconductor processes are not feasible for the purpose of fabrication, and if used, can compromise the device performance. Poor dimensional stability with varying processing temperatures limits the patterning resolution, causing overlay registration or alignment problems that restrict the scalability of flexible electronics. Surface roughness is also one of the important factors to be considered. Standard polymer substrates and transparent electrode materials, such as polyethylene terephthalate (PET) and indium tin oxide (ITO), have a high density of bumps and pinholes on the surface. This roughness renders the integration with the active layer difficult, degrades the device performance, and reduces the yield.

Owing to the advantages of nontoxicity, abundance, extraordinary optical and electronic properties, and a solution-based process, graphene-based materials have emerged as very attractive candidates for the applications of transparent and flexible UV sensors [5–7]. Herein, a prototype graphene-based nanostructure is fabricated on ITO-coated PET substrates using a low temperature solution-based process of spray-coating chemically reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for transparent and flexible UV sensing. Our work demonstrates the great potential of the graphene platform for large-scale, high transmittance, low cost, and flexible UV sensors.

Purchased ITO-coated PET substrates (I-one Film, South Korea) were first immersed and sonicated in a mixture of acetone, ethanol, and DI water with a ratio of 1:1:1 for 5min, blow-dried with nitrogen before being loaded into an ozone chamber for 30min under the illumination of intense ultraviolet light. These processes remove molecular organic contaminations and simultaneously change the ITO surface to a negative charge with oxygenated functionalization. A conventional photolithography technique followed by chemical wet-etching using hydrochloric acid (HCl 36% for 1min) was employed to produce the bottom ITO electrodes. Finally, rGO and GQDs dispersions were sequentially spray-coated onto the prepared ITO substrates to obtain transparent and flexible UV sensing. All the above processes are described in Fig. 1 . Further, the synthetic method of the rGO solution was mentioned in detail elsewhere [8]. High PL quantum yield N-doped GQDs (94%) were synthesized using citric acid (CA) and ethylene diamine (EDA) as precursors [9]. 0.21g (1mmol) CA and 0.18g (3mmol) EDA were dissolved into 5mL water, and stirred to form a clear solution. Then the solution was transferred into a 20mL Teflon autoclave, and the sealed autoclave was heated to 160°C in an electric oven and kept for 4h. The final product was collected by adding ethanol into the solution and centrifuged at 5000rpm for 5min, and then re-dispersed into water. The optical properties were investigated using photoluminescence (PL) and UV–vis measurement systems. The current-voltage (I–V) characteristics were measured under exposure to a 365nm UV light emitting diode (LED) with variable power using a semiconductor characterization system (Keithley 4200-MS Tech). The responsivity vs. incident wavelength was measured with a Xenon lamp (450W) and Oriel Cornerstone 130 1/8m monochromator as the light source.

The device fabrication processes are described in Fig. 1. It should be noted that spray-coating the rGO and GQDs dispersions was carried out at an extremely low temperature (120°C) to protect the ITO-coated PET substrate against deformation. Further, the rGO played a crucial role in generating a network between the ITO electrodes for electron propagation. Therefore, the reduction of GO must be sufficient to form rGO with an acceptable sheet resistance and optical transmittance. In our previous study, the spray-coating of GO dispersion was carried out with a preheated substrate at 250°C to form the rGO film [8]. In this work, we used an excess amount of hydrazine reducing agent and a longer reduction time, and the GO-hydrazine solution were heated to 90°C and stirred vigorously. We observed that this method can compensate for the low temperature spray-coating process of the rGO dispersion (120°C). The prepared rGO films still maintained a low sheet resistance of 2.2×103 Ω/sq and an optical transmittance of 84% at a reference wavelength of 550nm.

Fig. 2 (a) illustrates the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the synthesized GQDs, which are marked out in white dashed circles. It is evident that the prepared GQDs showed a mono-dispersed distribution without accumulation. An analysis of the size distribution spectrum in Fig. 2(a) reveals the uniformity in their size with diameters in the range of 6 to 7nm, with the most dominant size of 6.2nm. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 2(b) indicates the highly crystalline structure of the GQDs, stemming from the clear lattice fringes with an interlayer spacing of 0.21nm, as shown in the inset image.

In order to investigate the optical properties, the rGO and GQDs dispersions were gradually spray-coated on quartz instead of the ITO/PET substrate to avoid the absorption in the UV range of the PET substrate. The PL measurement of the rGO and the GQDs on the rGO samples was carried out using an excitation wavelength of 325nm (He-Cd laser source) with an output power of 10mW at room temperature (25°C). As shown in Fig. 3 (a), a blue peak intensity at 440nm with a full width at half maximum of 90nm has been observed for the GQD sample. Moreover, as shown in the inset, the dispersed GQDs in water clearly displayed blue emission under irradiation with a UV lamp. Our result was similar to another published paper about the PL characteristics of GQDs using the same He-Cd excitation laser source [10]. A possible explanation for this blue-shift is the quantum confinement and edge effects of the quantum dots. Our previous study indicated that the original luminescence of rGO is attributed to the localization of the electron-hole pairs of an isolated sp2 cluster embedded within the carbon-oxygen sp3 matrix [11]. When the GQDs were decorated on the rGO film, the blue emission was maintained but its intensity was greatly enhanced owing to the effective transfer of the photogenerated charges from the GQDs to the rGO film.

Fig. 3(b) illustrates the high optical transmittance properties of the rGO film and the GQD-decorated rGO film on the ITO/PET substrates. The photo in Fig. 3(b) demonstrates the high transparency of our device, which covers the rectangular area inside the 4 black dots. The black dots are drawn on the back side of the PET substrate to indicate the positions of the opposite ITO electrodes (Fig. 1), and to help connecting the electrodes to the probes of the semiconductor analyzer. It can be observed from these figures that for all the samples, the transparency is higher than 70% in the visible range (400–800nm). The transparency was slightly reduced after spray-coating the GQDs onto the rGO thin film. Notably, the rGO plays an important role in generating a network between the two bottom ITO electrodes for the photon-induced electron propagation as illustrated in the device structure of Fig. 1. Therefore, an adequate amount of rGO dispersion must be sprayed to fully cover the active size of 1×1cm2, as shown in the inset of Fig. 3(b). Consequently, the optical transmittance of the device was sacrificed for the sake of an improved electron transfer; thus, the larger the chip, the lower the transmittance.

Typical current-voltage (I–V) characteristics of the fabricated devices in the dark and under 365nm UV LED illumination are shown in Fig. 4 (a). A straight I–V curve was observed, indicating an Ohmic contact between the ITO electrodes and the GQDs/rGO platform. It also indicates that the rGO nanosheets overlapped one another to form a continuous film for carriers transfer. At a bias of 1V, the dark current was approximately 10.8μA, and when the detector was illuminated with a UV LED lamp (365nm, 1mW/cm2), the current was measured to be 12μA. An increase in the electrical current when the fabricated device was exposed to light is solid evidence of the behavior of the photoconductor. It indicates that the incident UV light creates an increase in the number of charge carriers in the active area. The UV absorption mechanism of the GQDs is related to the electron transitions from π to π* of aromatic sp2 domains and n-π* transition of CN and CO bonds [5,7]. In our experiments, spray-coating a higher amount of GQDs solution on the rGO film typically gives higher photocurrent but also higher dark current. The effect of the amount of GQDs solution on the electrical performance of our photodetector will be discussed in future paper. On the other hand, spray-coating a higher amount of rGO increase the film thickness, reduce the sheet resistance and reduce the optical transmittance [8]. Because of the lower sheet resistance, we observed a higher dark current, but no change in the UV photoresponsivity with thicker rGO films. Fig. 4(b) shows the photocurrent response of the GQDs/rGO-based UV sensor under different illumination powers. Another crucial characteristic of a photoconductor is the wavelength selectivity, defined as the wavelength of the optical light where the maximum responsivity is achieved. Fig. 5 (a) shows the responsivity of the transparent and flexible GQDs/rGO-based UV sensor under a bias of 1V at different wavelengths. It is evident that the fabricated device is sensitive to UV light from 300nm to 400nm and the maximum response is observed at a wavelength of 365nm. The device also exhibited a small responsivity in the visible region as compared to the UV responsivity.

In order to obtain a deeper insight into the working mechanism of GQDs/rGO film photoconductor, Fig. 5(b) illustrates the diagram of the carrier transport mechanism between the GQDs and rGO under UV illumination. S. Sarkar et al. reported that EHOMO and ELUMO of GQDs varied from −4.3 to −5.4eV and from −3.0 to −1.4eV, respectively [12]. These values were strongly affected by the size of the GQDs and the preparation method; whereas, the work function of the rGO film is known to fluctuate between −4.2 and −4.5eV [13,14]. The higher value of ELUMO of GQDs compared to the work function of the rGO film facilitates the photogenerated electrons moving from the GQDs to the rGO as described in Fig. 5(b). Upon UV light illumination, the electrons of the GQDs were excited to ELUMO from EHOMO by the absorption photon energy, creating electron-hole pairs, i.e. excitons, in GQDs. Zhang et al. proposed that there is a large probability that the excitons will be dissociated at the GQD interface [5], and then the electrons and holes will moved through the rGO film towards the opposite ITO bottom electrodes under the electric field, yielding the photocurrent. Lai et al. reported that a bias voltage applied to rGO nanosheets can effectively suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs, and increase the efficiency of extracting charge carriers to the electrodes [15]. Our results indicated that the GQDs played a major role in the absorption of UV and generation of carriers, whereas the rGO film acted as the electron pathway network. In addition, the extraordinary properties of GQDs and rGO, such as high transparency and flexibility, render this structure appropriate for UV sensing and a promising platform for the flexible optoelectronic device applications.

In conclusion, we have demonstrated a graphene-based platform for transparent and flexible UV sensing, consisting of spray-coated rGO and GQDs on an ITO-coated PET substrate. The process is inexpensive, low temperature and scalable; further, the device exhibits high transmittance and flexibility. I–V characteristics measurements under illumination by a UV LED (365nm) revealed that the device exhibits good photoconductive behavior. We proposed that our device paves the way toward efficient fabrication of transparent and flexible photodetector applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Basic Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2014R1A1A2008104).
